scenes.name: Title
scenes.text: Understanding what AI cannot do. [pause] Hallucinations, gaps, and the limits of memory. [pause] Knowing these is what makes you a careful user.

scenes.name: Hallucination
scenes.text: <emphasis>Hallucination.</emphasis> [pause] When AI produces text that sounds confident but is factually wrong. [pause] It's not a bug — it's a direct consequence of how language models work. The model generates plausible text, not verified facts.

scenes.name: Good at
scenes.text: What AI is genuinely good at. [pause] First drafts. Brainstorming. Explaining the same concept in different ways. Reformatting content. Translation. [pause] These are huge time-savers when used well.

scenes.name: Struggles
scenes.text: What AI consistently struggles with. [pause] Factual precision. Mathematical reasoning. Current events. Nuanced judgment. Consistency across long conversations. [pause] These are not edge cases. They are the model's structural limits.

scenes.name: Context
scenes.text: The context window is the AI's <emphasis>working memory</emphasis>. [pause] When a conversation gets too long, the earliest parts drop out. [pause] Practical move — provide critical information upfront, in one structured message. Don't rely on memory across many turns.

scenes.name: Strategies
scenes.text: Practical strategies. [pause] Always review AI output before using with students. Verify specific facts independently — dates, statistics, citations. [pause] When the topic is high-stakes, treat AI as a first draft, not a final source.

scenes.name: Teach it
scenes.text: Teaching students about AI limitations is itself a valuable lesson. [pause] It builds <emphasis>critical thinking.</emphasis> [pause] Students who know AI hallucinates become better evaluators of everything they read.

scenes.name: Closing
scenes.text: Your expertise. [pause] More important. Not less.
